item.title: Visa’s monitoring programs updated-VAMP Enhancements and Retirement of VDMP and VFMP
item.dc:creator: pm@kay
item.content:encoded: Effective on 31st March 2025, Visa will retire the Visa Dispute Monitoring Program (VDMP) and the Visa Fraud Monitoring Program (VFMP). And will enhance the Visa Acquirer Monitoring Program (VAMP) with effect from 1st April 2025. VAMP with the following changes:Oceanpayment VAMP Enhancements and Retirement of VDMP and VFMP 2025-06-25

item.title: iDEAL Operational Details For The Merchant Side
item.dc:creator: pm@kay
item.content:encoded: For websites that have already integrated the iDEAL payment method, even if they stop running, merchants must ensure that consumers can still browse normally for at least three months to prevent complaints from escalating due to consumers being unable to contact the merchant. iDEAL Operational Details For The Merchant Side

item.title: Implementation of Japan Credit Card Security Guidelines
item.dc:creator: pm@kay
item.content:encoded: Implementation of Japan Credit Card Security Guidelines 5.0

item.dc:creator: pm@kay
item.content:encoded: Guideline of OPCCOUNT

item.dc:creator: pm@kay

item.title: Recurring Payment Introduction
item.dc:creator: pm@kay
item.content:encoded: Oceanpayment Recurring Payment Introduction 2025-04-03

item.title: Klarna Bill Sample on the Customer
item.dc:creator: pm@kay
item.content:encoded: Klarna Bill Sample on the Customer-End 2025-04-03

item.title: Update on Mastercard Chargeback Reasons
item.dc:creator: pm@kay
item.content:encoded:
Mastercard plans to introduce an updated version of chargeback reasons starting from April 11, 2025. The updated version falls into the following major categories:
  • Authorization-Related Chargeback
  • Cardholder Disputes
  • Duplicate Processing
  • Chip Liability Shift-Lost / Stolen / Never Received Issue ( NRI ) Fraud
  • Chip Liability Shift
  • Questionable Merchant Activity
  • No Cardholder Authorization
Please check the details in Update on Mastercard Chargeback Reasons

item.title: Klarna’s APR Range Update for Consumers
item.dc:creator: pm@kay
item.content:encoded: We have been notified by Klarna regarding an update on the APR range for its financing products in the United States, which is designed to help more of merchants' consumers access flexible financing options, details as follows:[Oceanpayment] - Klarna’s APR Range Update for Consumers

item.title: Klarna Dispute Resolution Guideline
item.dc:creator: pm@kay
item.content:encoded: Oceanpayment-Klarna Dispute Resolution Guideline

item.title: Oceanpayment complaints and suggestions contact information
item.dc:creator: pm@kay
item.content:encoded: Complaints phone number 4006-290-296, select the transfer key “2 - customer service inquiries” or send emails to cs@oceanpayment.com.cn and leave contact information.

item.title: Do I need to re-apply when I change to a new domain for promotion?
item.dc:creator: pm@kay
item.content:encoded: Yes, please click “Apply” in ODPM - [Terminal Management] - [Site Management] - the following location to submit, in order to assist you to quickly apply.

item.title: If I need to activate a new payment method, how do I get the product price to activate it?
item.dc:creator: pm@kay
item.content:encoded: Please contact your dedicated customer relationship manager or Oceanpayment customer service at cs@oceanpayment.com.cn.

item.title: Can Oceanpayment support Shopify or other website builders?
item.dc:creator: pm@kay
item.content:encoded: Support Shopify, Shopyy, Shoplazza, Shopline, Xorder and many other SaaS website builders access, but also supports Magento, Opencart, Prestashop, Woocommerce, Zencart and other open source website building program access.

item.title: Does Oceanpayment support Mobile payment?
item.dc:creator: pm@kay
item.content:encoded: We support API access for PC, Mobile, TV and other smart terminals. For Mobile payment, we provide customizable SDK, which is compatible with iOS/Android system, supports 24 languages, and adapts to different sizes of devices.

item.title: Besides international credit cards, which countries' local payment products does Oceanpayment support?
item.dc:creator: pm@kay
item.content:encoded: It also supports local payment products in Europe, Southeast Asia, South America, Middle East, Russia, Japan, India and other countries and regions. More than 200 countries and regions can be covered.

item.title: What payment products does Oceanpayment offer?
item.dc:creator: pm@kay
item.content:encoded: The system platform supports more than 500 types of global payment products, covering more than 200 countries and regions, with payment types covering credit cards, e-wallets, Internet banking transfers, cash payments, cash-on-delivery, prepaid cards, carrier billing and so on.

item.title: What is a Chargeback/Retrieval/Fraud order? How should it be handled?
item.dc:creator: pm@kay

item.title: UK regulatory requirements for advertising Klarna
item.dc:creator: pm@kay
item.content:encoded: Based on the requirements of the UK government agencies to control credit products, Klarna has updated the requirements for the display of advertisements about Klarna in the UK region, the details of the policy requirements are as follows.

item.title: Why do I get high-risk order alert emails? How do I handle it?
item.dc:creator: pm@kay
item.content:encoded: Please see the guidelines below for details on the High Risk Order Alert emails.[ODPM Digital Platform] High Fraud Risk Alert Manual 2025-04-10

item.title: Mastercard Chargeback Assessment Criteria
item.dc:creator: pm@kay
item.content:encoded: 【风控】Oceanpayment——MasterCard Chargeback Assessment Standards 2025-04-10

item.title: What currencies are supported by Oceanpayment credit card channel?
item.dc:creator: pm@kay
item.content:encoded: 140+ currencies are supported, please contact your dedicated Operations Support Manager or Business Manager for more information.

item.title: The order is intercepted by the risk control to show 10000: Payment is declined, how to deal with it?
item.dc:creator: pm@kay
item.content:encoded: If the consumer is a trustworthy old customer, you can provide the consumer mailbox or card number to apply for adding a whitelist, if a new customer, it is recommended that the consumer to provide a scanned copy of the front of the credit card (can be blocked in the middle of the card number, and only retain the first 6, the last 4 digits), as well as personal documents scanned information to determine whether it is a trustworthy user, if trustworthy, then you can add a whitelist to the system, and if the system adds a block still you can contact your If the system is still blocked, you can contact your dedicated operation support manager to add the whitelist, and then if the order is rejected, you can keep the information for appeal.

item.title: What currencies can Oceanpayment settlement support?
item.dc:creator: pm@kay
item.content:encoded: Support CNY, USD, EUR, GBP, AUD, HKD, JPY, CAD, CHF, NZD, SGD and other settlement currencies.

item.title: How are funds from successful transactions settled?
item.dc:creator: pm@kay
item.content:encoded:
Upon fulfillment of the contractual settlement conditions, Oceanpayment will transfer the agreed settlement funds to the merchant's contracted bank account or Opasst settlement platform.
 
If the contracted account is a bank account, you can check the corresponding transferred funds in the bank account.
If you have signed with Opasst, you can login to the corresponding system to withdraw funds or make payment on behalf of the merchant.

item.title: I forgot my ODPM login password, how do I reset it?
item.dc:creator: pm@kay
item.content:encoded:
If you have forgotten the Administrator login password for your account, please contact your dedicated Operations Support Manager to reset the password.
If you have forgotten the password of the sub-accounts under your account, the Administrator of the account can reset the password.

item.title: Does ODPM support multiple account logins?
item.dc:creator: pm@kay
item.content:encoded: Yes, account super administrator can assign multiple sub-accounts in ODPM.

item.title: How do I keep track of my account funds and reconcile them?
item.dc:creator: pm@kay
item.content:encoded: Guideline of Reconciliation

item.title: Klarna Norway Newsletter Update
item.dc:creator: pm@kay
item.content:encoded:
Oceanpayment have received official notification from Klarna that as of August 28, 2024 Klarna will implement the following changes in accordance with the requirements of the Norwegian Consumer Authority Forbrukertilsynet:
1.Consumers must sign a regulated credit agreement for "Pay later in 30 days" similar to the existing Financing and Pay in 3 options.
2.Klarna will create a single framework agreement, signed once, covering all credit options.
3.Consumers will set their personalized credit limits.
These changes only impact Norway-based consumers. Customers will need to sign this agreement and set a credit limit to continue using Klarna's credit options. Pay Later options will remain interest-free, while Financing options will have a default 21.9% APR. Impact on merchants:
1.No action is required on merchant’s side.
2.Ensure Klarna is promoted using our on-site messaging feature to keep information updated.
3.Merchants are advised to refer to the following link to update their customer-facing FAQ with the latest Klarna details. https://support.oceanpayment.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/08/Klarna-Norway-Newsletter-Update-FAQ.docx
4.There may be an initial impact on conversion, but it should stabilize quickly.
Consumer Action:
1.Klarna will inform Norwegian consumers about the change and provide instructions for signing the agreement outside of the purchase flow.
2.Any existing orders or orders made before this is rolled out will not be impacted by this updated agreement, and will continue with their existing repayment schedules.
3.Consumers can adjust their credit limits anytime via the Klarna app or via customer support.

item.title: Oceanpayment Introduction of Prohibited Business
item.dc:creator: pm@kay
item.content:encoded: Oceanpayment——Introduction of Prohibited Business

item.title: What should I do with a risky and successful deal?
item.dc:creator: pm@kay
item.content:encoded: It is recommended that the consumer be contacted and asked to provide a scanned copy of the front of the credit card (the middle card number can be obscured, only the first 6 and last 4 digits are retained) as well as a scanned copy of the individual's identification (verifying that the name of the credit card's cardholder matches the name on the identification), and if the consumer is unwilling to provide this or the consumer can't be contacted, it is recommended that refunds be issued to deal with the situation.

item.title: UK Regulatory Requirements for Advertising Klarna
item.dc:creator: pm@kay
item.content:encoded: Based on the requirements of the UK government agencies to control credit products, Klarna has updated the requirements for the display of advertisements about Klarna in the UK region, the details of the policy requirements are as follows. Oceanpayment——UK-regulatory-requirements-for-advertising-Klarna-240131

item.title: Klarna Merchant Operations Guide
item.dc:creator: pm@kay
item.content:encoded: Ocenapayment ODPM - Operations Guide for Klarna Payment Method

item.title: How to add a blacklist or whitelist in ODPM?
item.dc:creator: pm@kay

item.title: ODPM Digital Platform Guideline
item.dc:creator: pm@kay
item.content:encoded: https://support.oceanpayment.com/en/wp-content/uploads/2025/06/Oceanpayment-ODPM%20Digital%20Platform%20Guidelines%20Manual.pdf

item.title: How to reduce the incidence of fraudulent and chargeback transactions?
item.dc:creator: pm@kay
item.content:encoded: Fraudulent transactions seriously affect the development of sellers and consumer experience ➢ Cross-border payment fraud is a problem that many cross-border e-commerce companies have encountered, and it also brings considerable losses to sellers; ➢ And the proportion of many sellers rejecting normal customers is also very high because they don't have the ability to judge the risk, all of these seriously affect the development of the business and the experience of customers. Therefore, we have organized some measures to prevent and control fraudulent transactions, please refer to the following guidelines; how to reduce the occurrence of fraudulent transaction refusal: Oceanpayment - Risk Control - Prevention and Control of Fraudulent Transactions 2022-02-24

item.title: Merchant Batch Representment Submission Guide
item.dc:creator: pm@kay
item.content:encoded: Oceanpayment-Merchant Batch Representment Submission Guide

item.title: Terms and Conditions
item.dc:creator: pm@kay
item.content:encoded: Terms and Conditions of the Oceanpayment Card_20251128163242 The Parties acknowledge and agree that cross-border or cross-currency transactions are exposed to exchange rate fluctuation risks. To cover such exchange rate risks and operational costs, the Company shall, at the time of settlement, levy an additional currency conversion fee on transactions involving currency conversion, at a rate of 1.2% of the total transaction amount. The aforesaid fee rate is not fixed and may be unilaterally adjusted by the Company in response to changes in market exchange rates.

item.title: Visa and MasterCard Refund Processing Rules Update
item.dc:creator: pm@kay
item.content:encoded:
1. Core Background of Rule Update

To bolster the security of refund transactions, enhance cardholders' refund experience, and mitigate fraud and chargeback risks, Visa and MasterCard have rolled out phased updates to their refund processing rules since 2019. Under the revised rules, acquirers and their processors must ensure all refund transactions are authorized online by the issuer before clearing submission.
This update only modifies the technical refund processing workflows on the acquirer side and issuer side. By subjecting refund transactions to real-time issuer authorization, it enables the interception of abnormal and invalid refunds at the source, effectively reducing subsequent chargeback incidents. Merchants can now monitor refund statuses in real time, proactively reach out to consumers with failed refund attempts, and complete refunds through alternative channels. Meanwhile, issuers are required to display pending refund authorization details to cardholders via online banking or mobile apps, allowing cardholders to track refund progress in real time.

Mandatory Effective Timelines

  • Visa: Fully implemented globally from April 18, 2020; fines for unauthorized refund transactions will be enforced starting July 1, 2020.
  • MasterCard: Effective from October 18, 2024; fines commence from May 1, 2025.

  1. Comparison of Refund Processes Before and After the Update
Previous refund rule (Direct clearing model)
New refund rule (Mandatory authorization model)
Core Workflow
Merchant initiates refund → Acquirer processes clearing → Issuer credits cardholder's account
Merchant initiates refund → Submit authorization request → Issuer approves → Clearing and posting
Status Visibility & Feedback
No real-time feedback; refund outcome only known after clearing
Real-time approval result (Approved/Declined) with corresponding reason returned immediately
Permitted Exceptions of Industries & Scenarios
/
Visa：Optional for airlines and mass transit merchants.
MasterCard：
  • Additional information on permitted exceptions is found in Section 5.11.3, "Obtain an Authorization," of the Mastercard Rules and Section 3.3, "Obtainingan Authorization," of the Transaction Processing Rules
  • Collections-only processing
  • Rebates
  • Airline transactions
  • Reversal transactions
  • Bridged transactions
  • Aggregated transit transactions
  • Merchant and acquirer funded installments

  1. Merchant Benefits

  • Improves customer experience and satisfaction
  • Reduces customer service inquiries related to lack of real-time information about a refund status
  • Provides real-time issuer account validation
  • Minimizes related chargebacks

  1. Actions for Oceanpayment Merchants

  1. No changes are required to your current refund workflow. Oceanpayment has already completed full system upgrades to ensure full compliance with card scheme requirements.
  2. Refund authorizations may be declined by issuing banks for reasons such as invalid account numbers, invalid account types, or expired cards. Merchants should track failed refund transactions, promptly reach out to affected customers, and perform the refund using another method when necessary.

item.title: Klarna Dispute Lifecycle
item.dc:creator: pm@kay
item.content:encoded:
1. No-Response-Required Dispute

Definition: When the Klarna customer files a complaint, Klarna will immediately notify the Merchant of the dispute. The disputed amount will be placed on hold at this stage, and Klarna will provide targeted guidance to the customer based on the specific dispute scenario:
  • Return the items following the Partner return policy in case of returns
  • Reach out to the merchant in case of other dispute reasons (goods not received, faulty goods and incorrect invoice)
Merchant Processing Timeframe: For general dispute scenarios, merchants are granted a 21-day negotiation window to communicate and settle with consumers directly without Klarna’s intervention. For “Unauthorized purchases”, however, merchants are only provided with a 3-day handling timeframe.
Dispute Reason
Timeframe for No-Response-Required
Return
21 Days
Good not received
21 Days
Faulty Goods
21 Days
Incorrect Invoice
21 Days
Already Paid
21 Days
High risk order
21 Days
Pandemic impact
21 Days
Unauthorized purchases
3 Days

  1. Response-Required Dispute

Definition: If the merchant and the consumer fail to reach a resolution within the No-Response-Required period, the dispute remains unresolved, Klarna moves into the investigation phase. The platform will first review the evidence submitted by the consumer to rule on whether the dispute is valid or rejected. Where the dispute is deemed legitimate, Klarna will send a request for further information, mandating the merchant to submit relevant documents for dispute defense.
Dispute Submission Limits and Timeframes：A consumer may file up to 3 disputes per order, with no restrictions on the dispute reason. Dispute eligibility timeframes are calculated from the date of its transaction time, as detailed in the table below:
Dispute Reason
1st Dispute (from capture)
Return
180 days
Good not received
180 days
Faulty Goods
180 days
Incorrect Invoice
180 days
Already Paid
180 days
Merchant Processing Timeframe: Merchants must respond within the required period. No further defense will be allowed if the deadline is missed.
Klarna Request Times
Merchant Response Deadline
First request
14 days (7 days for unauthorized transactions)
Follow-up request
7 days
There are different scenarios why an invoice gets disputed several times:
  • The customer wants to present new evidence to defend a dispute.
  • The customer disputes the invoice for different reasons.
  • Connected to a concern and reevaluation of the case.
The following special circumstances allow disputes to be initiated in excess of the number of limitations:
  • Unauthorized Purchases.
  • Legal claims raised via an external authority, such as the Central Arbitration Committee or National Board for Consumer Disputes.
  • Local legal consumer protection rules, i.e. the right to object in Sweden an agent is required to reopen a dispute again.

  1. Dispute Resolution and Closure

After receiving the necessary information from the merchant, Klarna’s support team will make a decision and resolve the dispute.
Result
Scenario
Klarna Status
Case Closure Procedures
Merchant Won
Customer:
  1. Is deemed to be at fault.
  2. Cannot provide the information requested by Klarna to proceed.
  3. Cancels the dispute on their own accord.
Resolved
The customer's invoice will be re-activated and dispute closed
Merchant Lost
Merchant:
  1. Is deemed to be at fault.
  2. Do not responds.
  3. Accept dispute.
Reversal-Merchant Lost
Merchant receives chargeback and dispute closed

item.title: Understanding Currency Conversion in Your Payment and Settlement Process
item.dc:creator: pm@kay
item.content:encoded:
The transaction currency set on the merchant's website directly affects the customer's payment experience and is integral to the funds settlement process. This guide explains how the chosen transaction currency affects currency conversion and exchange throughout the payment and settlement flow.

I. Currency Type Definitions

Currency Type
Description
Customer Payment Currency
The currency of the customer's credit or debit card, and the currency that appears on their billing statement.
Website Transaction Currency
The currency shown on the merchant's website checkout page, sent to Oceanpayment when the transaction is initiated.
Bank Channel Currency
The currency supported by the payment channel. Each channel typically supports multiple currencies (depending on the payment method), enabling the merchant to accept payments from key markets worldwide.
Merchant Settlement Currency
The currency agreed between the merchant and Oceanpayment for settlement, as specified in the contract.

II. Three Common Currency Conversion Scenarios

Currency conversion occurs when different stages of the payment and settlement flow involve different currencies. Below are three typical scenarios:
  1. Website Transaction Currency = Customer Payment Currency = Bank Channel Currency = Merchant Settlement Currency
No currency conversion is required at any stage in this scenario, delivering the optimal experience for both the customer and the merchant. However, the merchant must manage multiple currency accounts, which complicates fund consolidation. This option is suitable for merchants with multi-currency treasury needs.
  1. Website Transaction Currency = Customer Payment Currency = Bank Channel Currency ≠ Merchant Settlement Currency
Merchants typically unify their settlement currency to streamline fund consolidation. This approach balances operational efficiency for the merchant with a seamless payment experience for the customer. It is the recommended option and the most widely adopted by merchants.
  1. Website Transaction Currency ≠ Customer Payment Currency ≠ Bank Channel Currency = Merchant Settlement Currency
While this option meets the merchant's fund consolidation needs, it compromises the customer payment experience. Customers are subject to currency conversion during checkout, which increases the risk of complaints and disputes. Consequently, merchants generally avoid this option.

III. Application Scenario Examples

  1. Website Transaction Currency = Customer Payment Currency = Bank Channel Currency = Merchant Settlement Currency

Merchants targeting multiple countries or regions with multi-currency treasury needs may adopt the Multi-Currency Transaction + Multi-Currency Settlement model.
Example: A merchant serving the US, Sweden and Norway displays US Dollar (USD), Swedish Krona (SEK), Norwegian Krone (NOK) on its website based on the customer's location:
  • Customers pay in local currency with no conversion, for a smooth experience;
  • Oceanpayment processes orders in the respective currency (USD/SEK/NOK);
  • Oceanpayment settles with merchants in the same currency as transactions.
This model enables each market to operate entirely in its local currency, delivering the optimal payment experience with no foreign exchange involved; however, the merchant must maintain separate currency accounts for each market, resulting in fragmented funds and higher administrative overhead. It is therefore best suited for merchants with multi-currency treasury requirements.

  1. Website Transaction Currency = Customer Payment Currency = Bank Channel Currency ≠ Merchant Settlement Currency

Merchants targeting multiple countries or regions who need to balance customer payment experience with fund consolidation efficiency may adopt the Multi-Currency Transaction + Single-Currency Settlement model.
Example: A merchant serving the US, Sweden and Norway displays US Dollar (USD), Swedish Krona (SEK), Norwegian Krone (NOK) on its website based on the customer's location:
  • Customers pay in local currency without conversion for a smooth experience;
  • Oceanpayment processes orders in the respective currency (USD/SEK/NOK);
  • All transactions are converted to the merchant’s contracted settlement currency (e.g. USD) for fund consolidation.
Under this model, the currency displayed and charged is fully aligned with the customer's local currency, delivering the optimal payment experience; meanwhile, the merchant is relieved from managing multiple foreign currency accounts, as all transactions are ultimately consolidated into a single settlement currency, significantly streamlining treasury operations. This balance between customer experience and operational efficiency makes it the preferred option for most merchants.

  1. Website Transaction Currency ≠ Customer Payment Currency ≠ Bank Channel Currency = Merchant Settlement Currency

Merchants that target multiple countries or regions and prioritize fund consolidation may adopt the Single-Currency Transaction + Single-Currency Settlement model.
Example: A merchant with only a USD settlement account operates across the US, Sweden and Norway. Its website displays US Dollar (USD) as the only transaction currency in all markets:
  • Customers pay in local currency (USD/SEK/NOK). For Sweden and Norway, the issuing bank converts transactions to USD. Exchange spreads and conversion fees are borne by customers.
  • All orders are processed via banking channels in USD, regardless of the payment currency.
  • Oceanpayment settles in the contracted currency (e.g. USD) with no extra conversion, facilitating fund consolidation.
Under this model, the currency shown at checkout may differ from the currency appearing on the customer's billing statement. Customers bear both foreign exchange costs and exchange rate fluctuation risks, resulting in a suboptimal payment experience and increased potential for complaints. Consequently, merchants typically do not adopt this approach.

item.title: Merchant Final Settlement at Oceanpayment
item.dc:creator: pm@kay
item.content:encoded:
1. What is Merchant Final Settlement at Oceanpayment

Merchant final settlement at Oceanpayment (OP) refers to the process whereby, after a merchant terminates its use of Oceanpayment services, Oceanpayment settles all outstanding amounts payable to the merchant during the cooperation period. Upon completion of the final settlement procedure, Oceanpayment transfers the merchant's settlement funds to the merchant's OPCOUNT account at Oceanpayment. Once the funds have been transferred to the OPCOUNT account, all rights and obligations related to fund settlement under the acquiring services agreement between the merchant and OP shall be deemed fully concluded.

2. Oceanpayment Final Settlement Procedure

  • Given the characteristics of international credit card products, including chargebacks, the final settlement period for international credit card transactions shall be at least 180 days following the date of the last transaction.
  • For credit cards with special transaction liability period requirements, the final settlement timeline shall comply with the relevant rules of the card-issuing organization.
  • Merchants subject to AML sanctions are not eligible for final settlement.
  • For settlements conducted directly by the acquiring bank, the final settlement requirements of the acquiring bank shall apply.
  • If the service period promised by the merchant to consumers exceeds 180 days, the final settlement timeline may be aligned with the service period committed to consumers.
  • The merchant's KYC documentation is up to date: the company remains active, and there have been no changes in shareholding structure.
  • No negative fund transactions, including disputes, retrieval requests, chargebacks, or refunds, have occurred within the last 3 months.
  • At least 3 full calendar months have elapsed since the notification of card organization compliance control procedures.
  • If compliance penalties are applicable, such penalties shall be deducted from the final settlement amount prior to fund release.
  • AML screening shows no anomalies.

3. Rights and Obligations Upon Completion of Final Settlement

Merchant Side
All acquiring funds have been fully settled. The merchant is required to withdraw the funds from the OPCOUNT account at the earliest opportunity.
Oceanpayment Side
All funds have been fully settled. Any future chargebacks or fund fluctuations shall be borne solely by Oceanpayment.

item.title: Exchange Rate Fluctuations and Currency Conversion Fees
item.dc:creator: pm@kay
item.content:encoded: The Parties acknowledge and agree that cross-border or cross-currency transactions are exposed to exchange rate fluctuation risks. To cover such exchange rate risks and operational costs, the Company shall, at the time of settlement, levy an additional currency conversion fee on transactions involving currency conversion, at a rate of 1.2% of the total transaction amount. The aforesaid fee rate is not fixed and may be unilaterally adjusted by the Company in response to changes in market exchange rates.
